knowledge.title: First steps when a pod cannot reach another service
knowledge.body: When a pod cannot reach another service inside the cluster, start with DNS resolution before testing connectivity. Run `kubectl exec` into the failing pod and run: `nslookup <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. If the name does not resolve, the problem is at the DNS or service-existence layer — not the network layer. Debugging connectivity before confirming DNS resolution wastes time and masks the real cause.
knowledge.source_quote: The first thing I always check is whether the DNS name resolves. Most people go straight to curl or netcat, but if the name doesn't resolve you're debugging the wrong layer.

knowledge.title: Diagnose CoreDNS failures by return code, not just failure
knowledge.body: When `nslookup` fails inside a Kubernetes pod, the return code distinguishes the cause: NXDOMAIN means the service does not exist or is in the wrong namespace; SERVFAIL or a hanging request means CoreDNS itself is unhealthy. When CoreDNS appears unhealthy on specific nodes, check kube-proxy pod logs on those nodes — stale kube-proxy rules after a control-plane upgrade can cause DNS queries routed to CoreDNS pods on that node to fail while other nodes work correctly. Check that CoreDNS pods are running and review their logs alongside the kube-proxy logs on the affected node.
knowledge.source_quote: If it hangs or returns SERVFAIL, that's CoreDNS itself having a problem... check kube-proxy pod logs on the affected node if DNS failures appear node-specific.
knowledge.ambiguity_note: Reviewer clarified: node-specific DNS failures are more often stale kube-proxy rules than CoreDNS scheduling; original transcript conflated the two.

knowledge.title: Always use fully qualified service names when crossing namespaces
knowledge.body: Within the same Kubernetes namespace, a short service name resolves correctly. Across namespaces, short names silently fail. Use the fully qualified domain name in all cases: `<service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. Omitting the namespace suffix when calling cross-namespace services is a common mistake that produces silent DNS resolution failures with no error message indicating the real cause.
knowledge.source_quote: The moment you cross namespaces without the full name, it silently fails. I've seen engineers spend two hours on this.

knowledge.title: Adding the first NetworkPolicy to a namespace silently blocks all existing traffic
knowledge.body: Kubernetes NetworkPolicies are additive and default-deny once any policy exists in a namespace. Adding the first NetworkPolicy to a namespace that previously had none will block all traffic not explicitly permitted by that policy — including traffic that was working correctly before. This commonly affects services that were not mentioned in the new policy at all. When introducing NetworkPolicies into a namespace for the first time, audit all existing traffic flows and explicitly allow them before applying the policy.
knowledge.source_quote: NetworkPolicies are additive and deny-by-default once any policy exists in a namespace — so adding your first NetworkPolicy to a namespace silently blocks all traffic that wasn't explicitly allowed.

knowledge.title: Pod connectivity briefly fails after a node restart due to CNI reinitialisation
knowledge.body: When a Kubernetes node restarts, the CNI plugin reinitialises pod network interfaces. Pods that were running before the restart may lose connectivity to other pods on the same node for 30–60 seconds even after the kubelet marks them Ready, because the CNI has not yet fully restored the veth pairs. This is most visible with Flannel and Calico in VXLAN mode. The failure resolves on its own — do not restart pods to fix it, as that extends the outage. Monitor CNI plugin logs on the restarted node to confirm recovery.
knowledge.source_quote: When a node restarts, the CNI plugin re-initialises pod network interfaces... The symptom is pod-to-pod failures that resolve on their own within 30–60 seconds. Don't restart pods to fix it — wait.

knowledge.title: Retry-with-backoff must not be used on payment processing endpoints
knowledge.body: Standard retry-with-backoff logic must not be applied to payment processing endpoints. If a payment request succeeds but the response is lost due to a network timeout, a retry will issue a duplicate charge. The correct pattern for payment endpoints is to use idempotency keys so that retried requests with the same key are deduplicated server-side. Any HTTP client wrapper or retry decorator that is safe for idempotent endpoints is unsafe for payment endpoints without this protection.
knowledge.source_quote: A network timeout hit a payment endpoint, the retry fired, and the customer got charged twice because the first request had actually succeeded — the response just got lost.
